name: Offline Confidence Stamping & Sync (OCSS)
overview: OCSS enables disconnected operation with confidence-aware conflict resolution. Field operators can work offline with a local TrustDB instance, and changes are reconciled during sync using a 4-phase protocol that considers confidence levels, temporal freshness, and governance classification to resolve conflicts without data loss.

howItWorks.title: Phase 1: Departure Snapshot
howItWorks.description: Before going offline, the local instance takes a departure snapshot recording the current federation state vector. All offline writes are stamped with a 'disconnected' provenance marker and reduced confidence (configurable penalty, default 0.8x multiplier).

howItWorks.title: Phase 2: Offline Operation
howItWorks.description: The local instance operates independently, accepting writes with standard confidence envelopes. Temporal decay continues to run. The disconnected provenance marker ensures offline-created data is distinguishable from authoritative data.

howItWorks.title: Phase 3: Reconnection & Diff
howItWorks.description: On reconnection, the local instance computes a diff against the departure snapshot. Changes are classified into 4 categories: clean (no conflict), convergent (both sides made same change), divergent (conflicting changes), and novel (new data not in departure snapshot).

howItWorks.title: Phase 4: Confidence-Aware Merge
howItWorks.description: Divergent changes are resolved using confidence comparison: the version with higher current confidence (after decay) wins. If confidence is within a configurable tie-break threshold, both versions are preserved and flagged for human review via the conflict queue.

comparisons.narrative: Offline-first sync is well-addressed by CouchDB/PouchDB and CRDTs (Automerge, Yjs). These systems provide excellent automatic conflict resolution for text and simple data types. OCSS's differentiator is confidence-aware resolution — rather than last-write-wins or merge-all, it uses epistemic confidence to make resolution decisions. The tradeoff is more complex sync logic and the need for confidence metadata that CRDT-based systems don't require.

competitors.approach: Document-level revision trees with automatic sync
competitors.limitation: Last-write-wins or application-resolved conflicts; no confidence weighting

competitors.approach: Conflict-free data types that merge automatically
competitors.limitation: Limited to CRDT-compatible types; no confidence semantics; merge-all rather than choose-best
No offline confidence degradation; no conflict classification; binary online/offline
Manual conflict resolution; no automatic confidence-based resolution
